summary: We fled to Thailand during the war because we couldn't stay in our country anymore. After crossing the Mekong River, we reached Thailand and settled there for some time. I lived in Ban Vinai refugee camp in Thailand for 8 years, where I had three more children before eventually moving to America.

Crossing the Mekong River was one of the most frightening moments for me and my family. I carried my three children as we swam across, fearing the strong current and the presence of communist soldiers. Tragically, during the crossing, the baby carrier loosened, and I lost my youngest child in the river. It was a heartbreaking experience that I still remember vividly.

After arriving in Thailand, we moved to Nong Khai, which was farther from the riverbank. It was a place where we tried to rebuild our lives after the traumatic crossing of the Mekong River. From Nong Khai, I eventually went to live in Ban Vinai refugee camp for several years.

I lived in Ban Vinai refugee camp for eight years after fleeing to Thailand. During my time there, I had three more children and tried to create some stability for my family. The camp was a temporary home before we moved to America in 1988.

I am part of the Hmong people, and our community faced many hardships during the war. Our beliefs and traditions were important to us, like when my husband thought something spiritual touched his leg in the Mekong River. We fled our country together, crossing dangerous waters and living in refugee camps before resettling in America.
